explanation: TeamMessi prevails on logical coherence and persuasive force by anchoring the debate on the 2022 FIFA World Cup as football's most objective measure of individual greatness—a defensible framework that provides a clear, verifiable differentiator (Messi won as MVP; Ronaldo was benched in group stage). While both debaters deploy selective framing and rhetorical escalation, TeamMessi's logic remains more internally consistent: the World Cup claim is concrete and difficult to refute without dismissing the tournament's significance entirely. TeamRonaldo's strategy of invoking 'comprehensive excellence across decades' is conceptually weaker because 'greatness' itself is contested terrain; their repeated pivot to multi-league goal tallies and Champions League accumulation, while statistically impressive, cannot overcome the asymmetry that Messi achieved an objective trophy outcome Ronaldo never did. Evidence quality suffers in both cases due to abundant unverified claims (specific goal ratios, voting bias allegations, penalty conversion rates), though TeamMessi's invocation of tournament MVP awards and documented knockout-stage performances is slightly more verifiable. Clarity is comparable; both maintain articulate prose but resort to repetitive rhetorical cycles. TeamMessi's persuasiveness edges higher because the World Cup framing—while debatable as a singular metric—provides a concrete, universally-recognized conclusion, whereas TeamRonaldo's insistence that 'one tournament cannot override two decades' asks audiences to accept an abstract counterclaim without a clear alternative measure of supremacy.

factcheck_flags.claim: Ronaldo reached Euro 2016 finals and was instrumental; Portugal beat France with him as captain
factcheck_flags.issue: Misleading framing: Ronaldo was injured in the 25th minute and substituted at 50 minutes. He did not participate in the majority of the match or the final tactical structure. Portugal won, but primarily without his participation in the latter half.

factcheck_flags.claim: Messi won four Champions Leagues across Barcelona in 2009, 2011, 2015, and PSG 2024 qualification appearances
factcheck_flags.issue: Messi won exactly four Champions League titles: 2006, 2009, 2011, 2015—all with Barcelona. He did not win any Champions League with PSG and was not in PSG during 2024. The '2024 qualification' claim is fabricated.

factcheck_flags.claim: Ronaldo maintains elite performance at Al-Nassr averaging 0.92 goals per game
factcheck_flags.issue: This claim requires verification against Saudi Arabia league records. While Ronaldo has played for Al-Nassr, the specific 0.92 goals-per-game average is presented without verifiable source and may be inflated or taken from limited sample.

factcheck_flags.claim: Ronaldo's 140 penalties represent 15.7% of his 890 goals, an extraordinarily high ratio
factcheck_flags.issue: While the mathematical ratio (140/890 = 15.7%) is correct, the claim that this is 'extraordinarily high' requires contextual data on other forwards' penalty ratios that is not provided. The assertion that 'elite non-specialist forwards typically convert 8-12%' lacks cited sources.

factcheck_flags.claim: Messi won exactly two Champions Leagues while Ronaldo won four
factcheck_flags.issue: Factually false. Messi won exactly four Champions League titles (2006, 2009, 2011, 2015—all with Barcelona). This is one of the debate's clearest factual errors by TeamRonaldo.

factcheck_flags.claim: During 2008-2017 overlapping prime, Messi won Ballon d'Or in 2009, 2010, 2011, 2012, 2014, 2015 while Ronaldo won in 2008, 2013, 2016, 2017
factcheck_flags.issue: The list is incomplete. Ronaldo also won in 2013 and 2014. The actual 2008-2017 count appears to be Messi: 2009, 2010, 2011, 2012, 2015 (five or six, depending on overlap definition) versus Ronaldo: 2008, 2013, 2014, 2016, 2017 (five). The exact ratio claimed (6-4) requires verification.

factcheck_flags.claim: Messi failed across four World Cup tournaments (2010, 2014, 2018, 2022 group elimination)
factcheck_flags.issue: Messi did not experience 'group stage elimination' in 2022. Argentina advanced from the group stage and reached the final, where Messi won the tournament as MVP. This is a severe factual misrepresentation.
